THE ATTORNEYS' FEES PROVISION IN THE UNDERLYING CONSTRUCTION CONTRACT ENTITLES WEBCOR TO RECOVER "THE ACTUAL COSTS AND EXPENSES INCURRED IN CONNECTION WITH THE PROSECUTION OR DEFENSE OF SUCH ACTION AND ANY APPEALS IN CONNECTION THEREWITH, INCLUDING ACTUAL ATTORNEYS' FEES, EXPERT FEES, CONSULTANT'S FEES OR COSTS AND COMPENSATION FOR THEIR TIME SPENT BY IN-HOUSE COSTS." " '[W]HILE IT IS REASONABLE TO INTERPRET A GENERAL CONTRACTUAL COST PROVISION BY REFERENCE TO AN ESTABLISHED STATUTORY DEFINITION OF COSTS, WE DO NOT DISCERN ANY LEGISLATIVE INTENT TO PREVENT SOPHISTICATED PARTIES FROM FREELY CHOOSING A BROADER STANDARD AUTHORIZING RECOVERY OF REASONABLE LITIGATION CHARGES AND EXPENSES.' " (THRIFTY PAYLESS, INC. V. MARINERS MILE GATEWAY, LLC (2010) 185 CAL.APP.4TH 1050, 1065-1067 [AFFIRMING AWARD OF EXPERT WITNESS FEES PURSUANT TO LEASE THROUGH NORMAL PROCEDURES FOR REQUESTING AND TAXING COSTS].) Notice Of Motion And Motion To Tax Costs
Off Calendar Mar-06-2019 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

Appearances:
Donnelly a. Gillen, esq. (415-995-5096) of Hanson Bridgett for Plaintiff CDC San Francisco LLC.
Nicholas A. Merrell, esq. (415-623-7000) of VLMG LLP for Defendant WEBCOR CONSTRUCTION, L.P.

ARGUED. (as fully reported in the reporter's notes) The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

PLAINTIFF CDC SAN FRANCISCO LLC's Motion To Tax Costs

Plaintiff CDC San Francisco LLC's motion to tax costs is denied. Defendant Webcor Construction, L.P.'s entitlement to costs is governed by the parties' agreement, not by Code of Civil Procedure section 1033.5. The attorneys' fees provision in the underlying construction contract entitles Webcor to recover "the actual costs and expenses incurred in connection with the prosecution or defense of such action and any appeals in connection therewith, including actual attorneys' fees, expert fees, consultant's fees or costs and compensation for their time spent by in-house costs." " '[W]hile it is reasonable to interpret a general contractual cost provision by reference to an established statutory definition of costs, we do not discern any legislative intent to prevent sophisticated parties from freely choosing a broader standard authorizing recovery of reasonable litigation charges and expenses.' " (Thrifty Payless, Inc. v. Mariners Mile Gateway, LLC (2010) 185 Cal.App.4th 1050, 1065-1067 [affirming award of expert witness fees pursuant to lease through normal procedures for requesting and taxing costs].) (as fully reported in the reporter's transcript)

Order is signed in open court.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman, Clerk: S. Le, Court Reporter: Sherry Sawyer, CSR#5976, ssawyer@sftc.org, Reported. =(302/EPS)

DEMURRER TO 3RD AMENDED COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Dec-13-2018 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Scott E. Hennigh, Esq., Hanson Bridgett, Telephone 415-995-5010, appearing for Plaintiff;
Nicholas A. Merrell, Varela, Lee, Metz, & Guarino, LLP, Telephone 415-623-7000, appearing for Defendants' Webcor & Obayashi;

The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

Defendants Webcor Construction, L.P. and Webcor Construction, Inc.'s demurrer to all three causes of action in the third amended complaint is sustained without leave to amend as to all three causes of action. Plaintiff CDC San Francisco LLC has not alleged, nor does it appear that it is able to allege, ultimate facts showing that any of its claims are not time-barred. The August 24, 2018 Order limited leave to amend to two areas and required specific allegations for both areas. The allegations in the third amended complaint fall well short of what was required to successfully avoid the bar of the statute of limitations for each of CDC's claims. CDC is unable to allege a viable equitable estoppel argument based on misrepresentation of the identity of the licensed entity performing work on the hotel project. Interrogatories and pleadings from the construction defect litigation cannot be used to toll the one-year statute of limitations because these statements occurred in 2017, well after the statute of limitations ran in March 2010. Kleinecke v. Montecito Water District (1983) 147 Cal.App.3d 240 is distinguishable because in that case the defendant's tolling act occurred prior to expiration of the statute of limitations. Documents prepared and provided by third-party subcontractors and others referring to the name Webcor Builders, Inc. cannot provide a basis for estoppel because these alleged misrepresentations were not made by a Webcor entity. Most significantly, the judicially noticeable Notice of Completion of February 11, 2009 alone defeats CDC's argument that it did not become aware of the true legal name "Webcor Construction L.P. dba Webcor Builders" until 2017. Nor has CDC adequately alleged that any Webcor entity did unlicensed work on the hotel project within one year of the filing of this lawsuit. CDC's argument that bidding without the requisite license entitles CDC to disgorgement fails per the reasoning of MW Erectors, Inc. v. Niederhauser Ornamental & Metal Works Co., Inc. (2005) 36 Cal.4th 412, 435-36. MW Erectors held that disgorgement under section 7031 does not apply where an entity enters into a contract before obtaining a license if that entity is licensed at all times it performs actual work on the contract. The bidding activities described in CDC's third amended complaint necessarily occurred pre-contract and do not constitute the performance of work under a contract that triggers section 7031 liability. Order is signed.

Judge Harold Kahn; Clerk: William Trupek; Not reported. (302/HEK)

DEFENDANT OBAYASHI CORPORATION's DEMURRER TO FIRST AMENDED COMPLAINT, as to all three causes of action in the first amended complaint filed by plaintiff CDC San Francisco LLC is sustained with leave to amend. Leave to amend is GRANTED if CDC can allege in good faith ultimate facts showing that 1) any of the defendants performed unlicensed work within a year of CDC's filing of this case adn 2) CDC did not have constructive knowledge from public records of alleged unlicensed work performed by any of the defendants and did not discover that unlicensed work until one year or less before CDC filed this action.
